turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- this was a motion here verified -- verified and -- for these continuances. Now, this was not denied by the State of Indiana at all. He had no time --

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: Did he say there that he did not authorize?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: He denies them that there is truth of it.

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: Oh, say denied it but he didn't say -- he -- he did not say, as you read it, as I remember that he failed to authorize it. He did not authorize the newspapers to run it.

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: That was what I had in mind.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: The said press releases were given to the newspapers out of present defendant. The said releases were unsworn to and never seen by this defendant.

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: Well, of course, they -- they were his confessions. They -- they were bound to have been --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: There has been oral confessions if there's any or -- there's a vague confessions are, Your Honor, where there are oral confessions, there was never any written confession.

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: Well, was it written?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Never was a written confession (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: Well, they authorize an oral confession there, is that right?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: An oral confession. If there was anything it'd be in oral confession.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Lopp, as the -- the IM of the courier that -- your local paper, the Evansville paper, attributes to him a statement of his view --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: We deny that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what I am suggesting is what -- that what you have read may refer to his denial of the truth --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- or the authorization of what the authorities put out not what he put out. I listened with care to what you've read and I don't think my ears caught a denial that he himself gave some statement to the press. Is there such a specific denial of that?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, we try to get that across the Court in our motion for the continuance, Your Honor. In other words what we're saying to the judge, we don't just continue. Here the newspapers are out here on the streets, putting up these newspapers. And they're not true. That's what we end up saying. The said press releases are untrue, whatever it would be.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The press releases were the releases of the Chief of Police from the District Attorney --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, we mean -- we mean that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- of his confession to which he then made a reply and I think it is consistent with his denial of the truth that he -- of the -- of the confession attributed to him, it is consistent with his denial of the truth of that alleged confession to say that he didn't gave his version of the papers.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, he -- he doesn't -- we don't admit that. Those newspapers were attached here, Your Honor, to this motion for (Inaudible) continue but we deny that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: (Inaudible) Mr. Lockyear has found this for me on page 64. We tried to make this clear as we could. I -- that he had confessed to summation on said -- said morning that the date here was the confession were released and the purported statement on this defendant to the members of the press and radio when in fact this defendant made no such statements.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, there were -- there were good many items in the papers on successive days or they're not?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Right. That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Of course, they're in each of those, in each of those publications an attribution of something set by him on his own accounts?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, most of them were set with the prosecutor or the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Or then --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) I think that's one of the phrases I know of it that -- that Mr. Irvin heard in (Inaudible)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that that's one out of X --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- issues.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And at any event, it can cover this -- that the publications in which he didn't participate, is that right?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: He did not participate.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I say there were publication that and I --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- think Mr. Givan agreed --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: What --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- to that in which his name doesn't appear as the interviewed person.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: That's right. So Irvin did not authorize. We deny that Irvin authorized the publication to these things. And in -- when these -- the Court will disavow our objection here. We think it's a very good objection that the -- Mr. Weaver and the police officers nor anyone else had properly testified in this matter because this man was arrested, held in jail, he was arrested in Warrick County, mind you, by a State of Indiana police officer. And one other question I want to get across, one other thing here. Mr. -- Attorney, you were wrong. I feel sure. Irvin was not arrested for parole violation. Irvin was now -- at page 119, he was arrested for fraudulent checks and --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I understood him to say that.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: No --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That's what he did say.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) said he was arrested for parole violation, didn't you?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- which -- well, not a parole violation which does not -- in which it's not the -- the case at all.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You mean passing a bad check would not be a parole violation?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: But he was not arrested, part of the parole violation. The officer that arrested him -- arrested him for a fraudulent check. As a matter of fact, in this brief here of mine, I've got the -- asking what he was arrested for and he -- you -- this will be found -- here is the -- the examination of Weaver and who was the arresting officer. He was arrested for a parole violation. He was arrested for fraudulent checks.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, isn't that what -- isn't that what Mr. Givan said?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: But it specifically says in here, Your Honor, that he was not arrested for a parole -- parole violation.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, that says he was.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What you read was that he was. (Inaudible)

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Now, that's what Clevens says. He was arrested for (Inaudible) He was not arrested -- I'm sorry, let me go again. He was not arrested for a parole violation. He was not arrested for parole violation at all. He was arrested for fraudulent checks.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Was he out on parole at the time?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: He was out on parole. He was working at -- in Warrick County, steam city man a -- in project there, building some kind of a car plant, and he had been gainfully employed for a number -- well, since he's been out, and he -- he was a regular employee at that time. And they took -- they arrest him up there in this Warrick County which he joins Vanderburgh County. As they take him to the sheriff of that county or whatnot, they brought him back to the City of Evansville and he brought -- took him to the city police station. They did not take him to the sheriff of Vanderburgh County at all. They kept him there from April the 8th to April 14th and finally on April the 12th, was when the alleged confession was proof to have been given but he was not taken to the state judge until by April 14th. And then he was not appointed an attorney in this matter until April the -- on April 21st, he was indicted and rain -- arraigned, April 21st, he was arraigned and the Court on the April 22nd asked Irvin if he had an attorney, he said, no. And then the matter of arraignment was continued until April 28th when the Court appointed Attorney Robert Hay to represent him. So you've been readily see there a period of about 20 days there that he was not represented.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well in what proceeding was the lawyer appointed?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: The lawyer was appointed at the time of the period on the arraignment after the indictment was being returned, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: On arraignment for murder.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: On arraignment for murder, after the grand jury met.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: He didn't have any attorney when the grand jury met at all. And that juries I read to you here he says that he didn't appoint an attorney for him. He didn't have the attorney. And here was the -- the city jail and if you can just go right on one door right is -- the next is the -- is the city courtroom.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: If he -- if he was arrested as a parole violator, would it be essential to appoint an attorney for him?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: I think that if he was arrested for fraud, I think you can arrest anybody, I think you're entitled for an attorney. You're entitled to be informed the nature of the charge against him and -- and such, I think so. I believe it would be.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is he entitled as a parole violator to a trial in the courts there?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: I'm sure, he's entitled to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is he?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- trial court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: As a parole violator? I didn't know that. I --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: He is entitled to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- I thought --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) whether or not he would be entitled to a hearing on whether or not he was violating his parole.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, who does that --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Why he (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- in your State?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- to be brought --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- is that the Court or is that your board of prison directors or --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, he (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- or what agent -- what agency determines whether he's a parole violator? Is it the courts?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: I think that it -- it could be the courts. Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, is it done in your --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, not --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- procedure?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: I -- I don't --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You're a public defender I would --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- I don't want to get myself in the middle. I want to be perfectly fair here. I think it could be done in several ways. In other words, if he did not violate his parole, he didn't do anything at all. I think that he would be entitled to -- that the parole officer had him mocked up.q (Inaudible) he can file petition for writ of habeas corpus to get out if we didn't do anything about this parole. Other than that, I -- he was being assured, he'd been entitled to hearing before the parole board. Now, I -- other than that, I just wouldn't want to comment, Your Honor. I just wouldn't because I -- I wanted the Court to know that he did stand there on that time. I'm (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: You haven't referred -- you haven't referred to Dowd to a point that you make that as I understand it that the -- those were the questions of the exclusion of the rest of the panel while the examination of the voir dire was going (Inaudible)

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Yes, during the part of the -- that's found I think --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: I know what the point is but would you care to comment on it?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Yes. During the period of the voir dire examination here, Mr. Lockyear and I made a motion for this Court separating the jurors as they took the stand. And that is found on page 2399 of this transcript where we move the Court to separate these prospective jurors so that when they were in the jury box, they wouldn't be carrying up one or the other had to say. Court overruled our objection and refused to do that.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: What prejudice is subject to that?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: What we -- subject?

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: What's your point, yes. I didn't understand --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Our point is that is here, jury after jury and have a jury sitting in the jury box and jury after juror would come in, prejudice I think he's guilty. He might be related, he might be an aunt, uncle or brother or some -- one of the jurors already on there. There is one juror who thinks, "Well now, wait if John Jones thinks he's guilty, I know John Jones, why, maybe -- I'm thinking wrong here." And after so long of time, that's going to build us in your mind if you keep saying somebody is guilty, somebody is guilty 431 times you're almost, why, you're going to be -- it's not too hard to confess somebody that -- not just party on you.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: What did you want? Did you want for me to -- as soon as jurors would qualify, take them separately.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Take them, put them into another room.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And keep also prospective jurors out and only the one being examined the presence tribunal.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But your -- your -- the core of your case is that there are set on this jury X number of jurors, I don't know how many, three maybe. But there were at least three men on that jury, proved on detail examination, indicated that they had a settled conviction that this man was guilty but when taking in hand by the judge, and ask whether they could, despite that fact, render a verdict on the evidence, they said they thought they could. And your point is that that is -- that adds up to a humanly impossible undertaking, is that your case?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: That's right. In other words, the judge might --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No matter how this thing got into the papers, no matter how wise or unwise it is to allow papers to be printed, no matter who is responsible for it, the effect on the minds of those jurors were such that they couldn't rub it out. No matter what honest, self-deluding protestations they made or the assurance since they gave what they could.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: It could --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: On that point, I see neither one of you have referred to the case of Spies or Thieves against Illinois, have you?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: We have that in our brief.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: You have?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: -- in our brief.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What is the difference between the two cases?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, I -- on that Spies case, what you said and I remembered also, the jurors had not expressed their opinions. Now, in this case, these jurors had expressed their opinions and some of them, to the extent of everything except fixed stability.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I thought --

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: They couldn't make up their minds whether they want to give him death or life.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I thought some of these have gone along way in the Spies. I'm not meaning to say that Spies covers your case or that it should cover it but some of the jurors went a long way with reference to the statement that they were conveyed. And it was only by much finding of the Court with a number of instances that there could be urged to say that they did not have a (Inaudible) opinion.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: They didn't express to the third person or something because I think it's (Inaudible)

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: They expressed it in the Court.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Then the Court did it, yes.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And they were accepted on the jury.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Yes. But I think irrespective or as far as our Constitution comes first, the question is whether the due process, not a matter of qualifying him under some particular statute. It's qualifying him under the Due Process Clause of the Constitution. That's our position in this manner.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, (Inaudible) it's -- if the qualification and not the statute, then it becomes a question of whether the statute itself is constitutionally valid for due process reasons, isn't that right?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Well, yes.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Or as applied.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Its application of it.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Or as applied.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Yes. Now, we do have or we couldn't continue. I think my time is up.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Are you the public prosecutor, this is a matter -- are you the public defendant?

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Am I the public defendant?

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Or do you -- you were assigned discretion.

turns.name: JAMES D. LOPP
turns.text: Yes. I've been assigned for discretion.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Lopp and Mr. Lockyear, the -- on behalf of the Court, I like to express our appreciation to both of you for representing this man. We understand that you're appointed by the court below and had carried it through to this Court and without compensation to yourselves and we appreciate that kind of public service. And Mr. Givan, we want to express our appreciation to you for your frank and fairness presentation of the interest of your statement.
